title: Rearranging simple and compound sentences
description: Identify and arrange simple and compound sentences.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _simple_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The simple sentence is

>**The rhinoceros lowered its head and charged.
**

choices.content: The rhinoceros lowered its head and charged.
The rhinoceros lowered its head to charge, and the baboons scattered.
Lowering its head, the rhinoceros charged; a flock of birds got out of the way.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _compound_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**The venus fly trap snapped shut, but the nearby bug narrowly escaped being caught.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a comma and the coordinating conjunction "but".

choices.content: The venus fly trap snapped shut, but the nearby bug narrowly escaped being caught.
The venus fly trap snapped shut, catching a bug.
The venus fly trap snapped shut and caught a bug.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _simple_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The simple sentence is

>**I don't want to go inside this cave full of bats.**

choices.content: I don’t want to go into this cave—I think it’s full of bats!
This cave is full of bats; I don't want to go inside!
I don't want to go inside this cave full of bats.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _compound_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**Juniper went kayaking in the ocean, and she saw a humpback whale.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a comma and the coordinating conjunction "and".

choices.content: Juniper went kayaking in the ocean, and she saw a humpback whale.
While kayaking in the ocean, Juniper saw a humpback whale.
Juniper went kayaking in the ocean and saw a humpback whale.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _simple_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The simple sentence is

>**I have never been especially good at bowling.**

choices.content: I have never been especially good at bowling, but it’s never too late to learn!
I have never been especially good at bowling.
I have never been especially good at bowling, and I hate it.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _compound_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**Gizmo is scared of thunder; he hides under the covers whenever it storms.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a semicolon.

choices.content: Gizmo is scared of thunder; he hides under the covers whenever it storms.
Whenever it storms, Gizmo hides under the covers.
Gizmo hides under the covers when it storms due to his fear of thunder.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _simple_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The simple sentence is

>**Hagrid skillfully piloted his flying motorcycle over the skies of Surrey.**

choices.content: Hagrid skillfully piloted his flying motorcycle over the skies of Surrey.
Hagrid loved his flying motorcycle; he skillfully piloted it over the skies of Surrey.
Hagrid loved his flying motorcycle, and he skillfully piloted it over the skies of Surrey.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _compound_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**Sweetie the bull stamped his feet, and he snorted all over the pen.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a comma and the coordinating conjunction "and".

choices.content: Sweetie the bull stamped his feet and snorted in the pen.
Sweetie the bull stamped his feet, and he snorted all over the pen.
Sweetie the bull stamped his feet in the pen, snorting as he did.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _simple_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The simple sentence is

>**The witches stirred their cauldrons with long-handled birch sticks.**

choices.content: The witches stirred their cauldrons with long-handled birch sticks.
Even though they had fancy steel ladles, the witches still stirred their cauldrons with long-handled birch sticks.
The witches stirred their cauldrons, and they used long-handled birch sticks.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _simple_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The simple sentence is

>**They make enormous pork sandwiches in this Iowa town!**

choices.content: This town in Iowa is renowned for its enormous pork sandwiches: the filling’s bigger than the bun!
They make enormous pork sandwiches in this Iowa town!
Although they’re not the biggest in the state, this Iowa town has some of the largest pork sandwiches around!

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the option that best combines these sentences into a logical _compound_ sentence.**

> Stina is extraordinarily good at playing billiards. I've never dared to play with her.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We're being asked to create a **compound** sentence:

* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**Stina is extraordinarily good at playing billiards, so I've never dared to play with her.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a comma and the coordinating conjunction "so".

choices.content: Stina is extraordinarily good at playing billiards, so I've never dared to play with her.
Stina is extraordinarily good at playing billiards, I've never dared to play with her.
I've never dared to play with Stina; really, extraordinarily good at playing billiards.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the option that best combines these sentences into a logical _simple_ sentence.**

> We wore straw hats to shield our eyes. The sun was blistering.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We're being asked to create a **simple** sentence:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.

The simple sentence is

>**We wore straw hats to shield our eyes from the blistering sun.**

choices.content: We wore straw hats to shield our eyes, and the sun was blistering.
We wore straw hats to shield our eyes from the blistering sun.
The sun was blistering; we wore straw hats to shield our eyes.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the option that best combines these sentences into a logical _compound_ sentence.**

> Nikolai waited very carefully in the bushes. He wanted to take a photo of the baby bear.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We're being asked to create a **compound** sentence:

* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**Nikolai waited very carefully in the brush; he wanted to take a photo of the baby bear.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a semicolon.

choices.content: Nikolai waited very carefully in the bushes to take a photo of the baby bear.
Nikolai waited very carefully in the bushes, he wanted to take a photo of the baby bear.
Nikolai waited very carefully in the bushes; he wanted to take a photo of the baby bear.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Combine the two sentences to make a logical _simple_ sentence.**

> The ponies trotted on the tundra.  They were unaware of the wolves.


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We're being asked to create a **simple** sentence:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.

The simple sentence is

>**The ponies trotted on the tundra, unaware of the wolves.**

choices.content: The ponies trotted on the tundra, unaware of the wolves.
The ponies trotted on the tundra; they were unaware of the wolves.
The ponies trotted on the tundra, and they were unaware of the wolves.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Combine the two sentences to make a logical _compound_ sentence.**

> I bought a delicious pie at an Amish country store. I think it’s got apples and peaches in it.


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We're being asked to create a **compound** sentence:

* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**I bought a delicious pie at an Amish country store; I think it’s got apples and peaches in it.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a semicolon.

choices.content: The delicious pie I bought at an Amish country store has apples and peaches in it.
I bought a delicious pie  with apples and peaches in it at an Amish country store.
I bought a delicious pie at an Amish country store; I think it’s got apples and peaches in it.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Combine the two sentences to make a logical _simple_ sentence.**

> Angelica likes candy. Her favorites are those little root beer barrels.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We're being asked to create a **simple** sentence:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.

The simple sentence is

>**Angelica’s favorite candies are those little root beer barrels.**

choices.content: Angelica’s favorite candies are those little root beer barrels.
Angelica likes candy; her favorites are those little root beer barrels.
Angelica likes candy, and her favorites are those little root beer barrels.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Combine the two sentences to make a logical _simple_ sentence.**

> We stood within the circle of Stonehenge. We watched the moon rise.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We're being asked to create a **simple** sentence:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.

The simple sentence is

>**We stood within the circle of Stonehenge and watched the moon rise.**

choices.content: We stood within the circle of Stonehenge and watched the moon rise.
We stood within the circle of Stonehenge, and we watched the moon rise.
The moon rose, and we stood within the circle of Stonehenge.
